title.value: OTC: Optimal Tool Calls via Reinforcement Learning
title.evidence: OTC: Optimal Tool Calls via Reinforcement Learning

authors.evidence: Hongru Wangα, Cheng Qianβ, Wanjun Zhongδ, Xiusi Chenβ, Jiahao Qiuσ, Shijue Huangµ, Bowen Jinβ, Mengdi Wangσ, Kam-Fai Wongα, Heng Jiβ

value: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
Hong Kong University of Science and Technology
affiliations.evidence: αThe Chinese University of Hong Kong, βUniversity of Illinois Urbana-Champaign σPrinceton University, $^ \delta \mathsf { S u n }$ Yat-sen University, $\mu$ Hong Kong University of Science and Technology

current_issues.value: Existing approaches often overlook the efficiency and cost associated with tool usage, leading to excessive tool calls that increase computational and financial overhead.
current_issues.evidence: While reinforcement learning (RL) has shown promise in improving TIR by optimizing final answer correctness, existing approaches often overlook the efficiency and cost associated with tool usage. This can lead to suboptimal behavior, including excessive tool calls that increase computational and financial overhead...

target.value: Develop a method that balances answer correctness with efficient and contextually appropriate tool usage.
target.evidence: We aim to develop such a method based on a foundational assumption: for each question and each model, there exists an optimal number of tool calls, defined as the minimal number required for the model to arrive at a correct answer.

framework_innovation.value: Introduced Optimal Tool Call-controlled Policy Optimization (OTC-PO), a framework that encourages efficient tool usage.
framework_innovation.evidence: We propose Optimal Tool Call-controlled Policy Optimization (OTC-PO), a simple yet effective RL-based method that enables large language models (LLMs) to learn precise and adaptive control over external tool usage.

metric_innovation.value: Introduced tool productivity as a metric to measure the effectiveness and efficiency of tool usage.
metric_innovation.evidence: We additionally define a new metric, tool productivity (TP), which measures the effectiveness and efficiency of tool calls during inference.

algorithm_innovation.value: Implemented OTC-PPO and OTC-GRPO, two variants of the OTC-PO framework.
algorithm_innovation.evidence: We instantiate this framework within both Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) and Group Relative Preference Optimization (GRPO), resulting in OTC-PPO and OTC-GRPO.

performance_improvement.value: Achieved up to 73.1% reduction in tool calls and 229.4% improvement in tool productivity while maintaining comparable answer accuracy.
performance_improvement.evidence: Experiments with Qwen-2.5 and Qwen-Math across multiple QA benchmarks show that our approach reduces tool calls by up to 73.1% and improves tool productivity by up to 229.4%, while maintaining comparable answer accuracy.

tool_call_reduction.value: OTC-PO reduces tool calls by up to 73.1%.
tool_call_reduction.evidence: Experiments with Qwen-2.5 and Qwen-Math across multiple QA benchmarks show that our approach reduces tool calls by up to 73.1%.

model_size_effect.value: Larger models tend to over-rely on external tool calls unless explicitly penalized for such behavior.
model_size_effect.evidence: As the model size increases, the TC and TP get bigger boost no matter in OTC-PPO or OTC-GRPO.

generalization_performance.value: OTC-PO maintains comparable accuracy across various model sizes and tasks.
generalization_performance.evidence: We highlight the ii) and iv) are very important since they compose the great potential scalability for our methods as model scales.

case_study_insights.value: OTC-PO achieves correct answers with fewer tool calls compared to baselines.
case_study_insights.evidence: Our method achieves same answers with baseline using fewer tool calls in mostly 80% of test cases (i.e., A).

task_definition.value: Generate the correct answer with the minimal cost of tools in the reasoning trajectory.
task_definition.evidence: The objective of the task is to generate the correct answer $\hat { a }$ with the minimal cost of tools in the full trajectory.

base_reward.value: 1 if the answer is correct, otherwise 0.
base_reward.evidence: Most prior predominantly focuses on rule-based correctness rewards.

efficiency_reward.value: Encourages fewer tool calls through a scaling coefficient.
efficiency_reward.evidence: We introduce a tool-integrated reward that modulates traditional reward signals with a scaling coefficient reflecting tool efficiency.

final_reward.value: The final reward combines the correctness reward with the tool efficiency reward.
final_reward.evidence: We regard the tool-integrated reward $r _ { t o o l }$ as a coefficient of conventional reward function $r _ { \phi } ( q , y )$.

algorithm_integration.value: Compatible with various RL algorithms, implemented in OTC-PPO and OTC-GRPO.
algorithm_integration.evidence: We note that it is compatible with various RL algorithms and can be easily implemented with only a few line changes.

datasets.value: NQ and HotpotQA for search; ToRL dataset for code.
datasets.evidence: Specifically, we use NQ and HotpotQA as training dataset for search, and we use the dataset provided in ToRL for code.

models.evidence: Using multiple LLMs—including Qwen-2.5-3B/7B-Base and Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B/7B-Base, we show that OTC-PO significantly reduces tool calls during reasoning trajectories while maintaining comparable accuracy to conventional approaches.

parameters.value: Max turns for tool calls set to 4 for Search-R1 and 3 for ToRL.
parameters.evidence: We set the max turn (maximal number of tool calls) in Search-R1 (ToRL) to 4 (3) to better study the effects of our methods.

resources.evidence: We conduct our experiments on 8 A100-80G GPU and re-produce the results of Search-R1 and ToRL independently.

search_tool_results.value: OTC-PO outperforms Search-R1 in terms of tool calls and productivity.
search_tool_results.evidence: OTC-PPO achieves significant improvement in terms of TC and TP compared with Search-R1.

code_tool_results.value: Preliminary results show similar findings for code tasks.
code_tool_results.evidence: We already observe similar findings as mentioned above and will release more results soon.

behavior_analysis.value: OTC-PO reduces unnecessary tool calls and improves training efficiency.
behavior_analysis.evidence: Our method not only achieves comparable results with fewer tool calls and shorter responses, but also enables faster and more efficient training optimization.
